questions.question: Match each PFC sub-region to its core function:
categories: Top-down cognitive control, working memory manipulation
Inhibiting responses, working memory maintenance
Reward-based decisions, reversal learning
Value-based / moral decisions, social behaviour
Most anterior; relating rules, abstract reasoning

correct_mapping.dlPFC: Top-down cognitive control, working memory manipulation
correct_mapping.FPC: Most anterior; relating rules, abstract reasoning

questions.explanation: The PFC sub-region map: dlPFC handles manipulation and abstract rule application, vlPFC handles maintenance and inhibition, OFC handles reward/reversal, vmPFC handles moral/social value, FPC sits most anterior for relating rules and goal hierarchies.

questions.question: Match each historical figure to their contribution:
categories: Phrenology — wrong, but encouraged localisation research
Posterior frontal stimulation drives contralateral movement (1860s)
Bilateral PFC lesions in monkeys cause loss of initiative + disorganised behaviour
Estimated PFC ~30% of human cortex (1912)

correct_mapping.Franz Gall: Phrenology — wrong, but encouraged localisation research
correct_mapping.Hitzig & Fritsch: Posterior frontal stimulation drives contralateral movement (1860s)
correct_mapping.Ferrier & Bianchi: Bilateral PFC lesions in monkeys cause loss of initiative + disorganised behaviour

questions.explanation: These four anchor the historical lineage: Gall (phrenology), Hitzig & Fritsch (motor cortex stimulation), Ferrier & Bianchi (bilateral PFC lesion effects in monkeys), and Brodmann (early PFC scaling estimates later refined by Semendeferi).

questions.question: Match each lesion syndrome to its hallmark:
categories: Apathy, no planning, abulia (lateral PFC)
Manic affect, social-cue failure, profanity (ventral/medial PFC)
Knows the rules but acts impulsively (vmPFC, Damasio)
Imitation + utilization behaviour (Lhermitte)

correct_mapping.Frontal disinhibition syndrome: Manic affect, social-cue failure, profanity (ventral/medial PFC)
correct_mapping.Acquired sociopathy: Knows the rules but acts impulsively (vmPFC, Damasio)

questions.explanation: Lateral PFC damage = dysexecutive (apathy/abulia); ventral/medial damage = disinhibition; severe vmPFC damage = Damasio's acquired sociopathy; anterior/medial damage = Lhermitte's environmental dependency with imitation and utilization behaviour.

questions.question: Match each task to its primary inhibition demand and key region:
categories: Detect rare stimulus → P300 ERP at ~300 ms
Inhibit prepotent response on rare 'no-go' (impaired in schizophrenia)
Cancel an already-initiated response → right vlPFC (impaired in ADHD, Parkinson's)

correct_mapping.Oddball task: Detect rare stimulus → P300 ERP at ~300 ms
correct_mapping.Go/No-Go task: Inhibit prepotent response on rare 'no-go' (impaired in schizophrenia)
correct_mapping.Stop-signal task: Cancel an already-initiated response → right vlPFC (impaired in ADHD, Parkinson's)

questions.explanation: Three inhibition task families: oddball produces the P300 ERP for detecting rare stimuli, go/no-go indexes prepotent inhibition (impaired in schizophrenia on no-go trials), and stop-signal requires cancelling an in-flight response — selectively impaired by right vlPFC damage, in ADHD, and in Parkinson's.

questions.question: Match each basal ganglia loop to its cortical input and function:
categories: Primary, premotor, SMA cortex — planning + executing movement
dlPFC — planning complex behaviour, decision making, social moderation
ACC, OFC — emotion, mood, reward

correct_mapping.Motor loop: Primary, premotor, SMA cortex — planning + executing movement
correct_mapping.Prefrontal loop: dlPFC — planning complex behaviour, decision making, social moderation

questions.explanation: Three basal ganglia loops (Figure 13.7): motor (M1/premotor/SMA → movement), prefrontal (dlPFC → decision making), and affective (ACC/OFC → emotion and reward). Damage to BG loops can produce abulia, the loss of spontaneous action.

questions.question: Match each researcher/team to their key contribution:
categories: PFC = railroad switch operator metaphor
Rule-selective neurons in lateral PFC, independent of cue/stimulus
PFC patients show perseveration on WCST
Frontal damage destroys the abstract attitude

correct_mapping.Wallis, Anderson & Miller (2001): Rule-selective neurons in lateral PFC, independent of cue/stimulus
correct_mapping.Milner (1963): PFC patients show perseveration on WCST
correct_mapping.Goldstein & Scheerer (1941): Frontal damage destroys the abstract attitude

questions.explanation: Four foundational contributions: Miller & Cohen's switch-operator metaphor, Wallis et al.'s single-unit demonstration of distributed rule coding, Milner's perseveration finding, and Goldstein & Scheerer's abstract-attitude concept (e.g., refusing 'the snow is black' as untrue).

questions.question: Match each rostral-caudal gradient theory and finding:
categories: Cascade — premotor → mid-dlPFC → anterior PFC by temporal abstraction
Policy abstraction hierarchy across lateral PFC
Deductive reasoning = vlPFC; inductive reasoning = dlPFC
FPC activates specifically for relational integration

correct_mapping.Koechlin: Cascade — premotor → mid-dlPFC → anterior PFC by temporal abstraction
correct_mapping.Badre & D'Esposito: Policy abstraction hierarchy across lateral PFC
correct_mapping.Goel & Dolan (2004): Deductive reasoning = vlPFC; inductive reasoning = dlPFC
correct_mapping.Smith, Keramatian & Christoff (2007): FPC activates specifically for relational integration

questions.explanation: Two rostral-caudal frameworks (Koechlin's temporal cascade vs Badre & D'Esposito's policy hierarchy), plus key reasoning dissociations (Goel & Dolan: deduction vs induction) and the FPC-relational-integration finding (Smith et al. 2007).

questions.question: Match each dmPFC region to its task type (Venkatraman et al. 2009):
categories: Response-related control (counting Stroop)
Decision-related control (Pochon & Cohen face attractiveness)
Strategy-related control (deviating from baseline strategy)
questions.explanation: Venkatraman et al. (2009) used three conflict tasks in one fMRI/EEG session and mapped them onto a posterior-to-anterior dmPFC gradient (response → decision → strategy), mirroring the dlPFC gradient. Taren et al. (2011) later confirmed topographic medial-lateral connectivity.

questions.question: Match each working memory study/concept to its key claim:
categories: Sustained, content-specific delay-period firing in dlPFC; memory fields
vlPFC = maintenance; dlPFC = manipulation
MVPA decoded remembered orientation from V1-V4
Single LTM store with activated reps; ~4-item focus of executive control

correct_mapping.Funahashi, Bruce & Goldman-Rakic (1989): Sustained, content-specific delay-period firing in dlPFC; memory fields
correct_mapping.Harrison & Tong (2009): MVPA decoded remembered orientation from V1-V4
correct_mapping.Cowan (1998): Single LTM store with activated reps; ~4-item focus of executive control

questions.explanation: The four pillars of WM neuroscience covered: Funahashi et al. (delay-period firing), Petrides (maintenance/manipulation dissociation), Harrison & Tong (sensory cortex stores content), and Cowan's model (favoured at neural level over Baddeley).

questions.question: Match each ACC/conflict finding to its source:
categories: Three control systems: alertness, orienting, detection of events needing extra resources
ACC trial N → dlPFC trial N+1 (conflict-then-control)
Dorsal ACC lesion patients have a normal Stroop effect
dmPFC tracks expectation mismatch / volatility — a learning signal

correct_mapping.Posner & Petersen (1990): Three control systems: alertness, orienting, detection of events needing extra resources
correct_mapping.Kerns et al. (2004): ACC trial N → dlPFC trial N+1 (conflict-then-control)
correct_mapping.Fellows & Farah (2005): Dorsal ACC lesion patients have a normal Stroop effect
correct_mapping.Behrens et al. (2007): dmPFC tracks expectation mismatch / volatility — a learning signal

questions.explanation: The arc of ACC theory: Posner & Petersen set up the three control systems (with conflict monitoring tied to ACC), Kerns et al. provided supporting fMRI evidence for the conflict-then-control hand-off, Fellows & Farah challenged it via lesion data, and Behrens et al. reframed dmPFC as a volatility/learning signal.
